segments.inferno-31-g31: Poi is an adverb meaning 'then' that places the action in sequence. Caramente is an adverb (here 'kindly' or 'tenderly', an older/poetic use) modifying prese; mi is the first‑person direct object clitic 'me'; prese is passato remoto (third person singular) of prendere, 'he took'. Per + mano is an instrumental phrase 'by the hand' (per = 'by/with' here). The coordinated e disse links this action to the following spoken clause ('and said:').
segments.inferno-31-g32: Pria che is a temporal conjunction 'before' that governs the subjunctive; che is part of that conjunction. Siam is the present subjunctive (first‑person plural) of essere used after prima/ pria che ('before we are/ before we go on'). Più avanti is an adverbial phrase 'further on' or 'any farther'.
segments.inferno-31-g33: Acciò che = 'so that/ in order that', introducing a purpose/result clause and normally demanding the subjunctive. ’l is the elided article 'il' before fatto; fatto here is a noun 'the fact/the matter' (literally 'that which is done'); men = 'less'; ti is the dative/clitic 'to you'; paia is present subjunctive (third person singular) of parere/paiare 'seem' agreeing with il fatto; strano is a predicate adjective 'strange'. Word order 'men ti paia' = 'seem less to you.'
segments.inferno-31-g34: Sappi is the second‑person singular imperative of sapere ('know!'), used here to give information to the addressee. Che introduces the content clause 'know that ...'. Non son is present indicative of essere in the contracted form (non + son = are not). Torri is the predicate noun 'towers' (plural).
segments.inferno-31-g35: Ma is the adversative conjunction 'but' (contrasting what follows with 'not towers'). Giganti is the noun 'giants' in the nominative plural and supplies the true identity after the contrast: 'not towers, but giants.'
segments.inferno-31-g36: E son repeats the copular verb 'and they are' (present indicative). Nel pozzo is a locative prepositional phrase 'in the pit/abyss' (nel = in + il). Intorno 'around' qualifies their position; da la ripa ('from the bank/edge') uses the older separation da + la instead of dalla. Da l'umbilico in giuso marks vertical extent 'from the navel downward' (l'umbilico = 'the navel/center'); in giuso = 'downwards'. Tutti quanti is a reinforced plural 'every one/all of them.'
segments.inferno-31-g37: This is a temporal/comparative clause introduced by 'Come quando' = 'Just as when'. 'la nebbia' is the subject (feminine sing.), 'si dissipa' is a pronominal verb (reflexive/impersonal) in the indicative present tense — literally 'dissipates itself' = 'clears away'.
segments.inferno-31-g38: 'Lo sguardo' (the gaze/view) is the grammatical subject; 'a poco a poco' is a repeated adverbial phrase meaning 'little by little'. 'raffigura' is 3rd-person singular present indicative meaning 'makes out / brings into view'. The relative clause 'ciò che cela 'l vapor che l'aere stipa' explains what the gaze makes visible: 'ciò che' = 'that which', 'cela' = 'conceals', and 'che l'aere stipa' modifies 'vapor' (the vapour which the air packs/crowds).
segments.inferno-31-g39: 'Così' links this clause to the preceding simile ('as ... so ...'). 'Forando' is a present participle of 'forare' = 'piercing/penetrating'; it starts a participial phrase modifying the main clause. 'l’aura' (here 'aura' = breeze) is followed by adjectives 'grossa e scura' = 'thick and dark' describing that air. Note: 'aura' is poetically used for 'breeze' rather than 'gold' (lemma given).
segments.inferno-31-g40: 'Più e più' = 'more and more'; 'appressando' (present participle of 'appressare') = 'approaching/pressing near' continues the participial action begun with 'forando'. 'ver'' is an aphetic form of 'verso' meaning 'toward' (here shortened with an apostrophe), and 'la sponda' = 'the bank/shore' is the goal of the approach.
segments.inferno-31-g41: Two archaic/enclitic perfective-imperfective verbs appear: 'fuggiemi' = 'fled from me' or 'error escapes me' (verb + enclitic pronoun -mi) and 'cresciemi' = 'grew in me' (similarly verb + -mi). Both are imperfect forms with an enclitic object pronoun. The sentence structure is subjectless for these verbs; the experiencer is indicated by '-mi'.
segments.inferno-31-g42: 'Però che' here introduces the causal explanation ('because/for that'), leading into the second half of the simile; 'però' functions as a subordinating causal particle followed by 'che'.
segments.inferno-31-g43: Another simile introduced by 'come' = 'as'. 'Su la cerchia tonda Montereggion di torri si corona' has an awkward poetic word order: 'Montereggion' (a place) is surrounded by a round rampart ('cerchia tonda'), and 'si corona di torri' means 'is crowned with towers' — 'si' is reflexive/passive marker, 'corona' = 3rd-singular present indicative meaning 'is crowned'.
segments.inferno-31-g44: 'Così' closes the comparison: just as Montereggion is crowned, 'la proda che 'l pozzo circonda' (the bank that surrounds the pit) 'torreggiavan' — an archaic imperfect plural (torreggiavano) meaning 'rose in towers / towered up'. 'Di mezza la persona' indicates extent: 'to half the person' (i.e., to half a man's height). Word order places the verb before the subject 'li orribili giganti' (the horrible giants), which is common in Dante.
segments.inferno-31-g45: Relative clause 'cui minaccia Giove del cielo ancora quando tuona' modifies 'li orribili giganti': 'cui' = 'whom', 'minaccia' = present indicative 'threatens' (Giove is subject), 'Giove del cielo' = 'Jupiter of heaven' and 'ancora quando tuona' = 'still when he thunders' (temporal/occasional). The sense is that even the thunderous Jupiter menaces these giants.
segments.inferno-31-g46: 'E io scorgeva già' = 'And I already perceived/made out'. 'Scorgeva' is the imperfect of 'scorgere' (to discern/spot), first-person singular past imperfect here describing a continuing perception. 'd'alcun la faccia' means 'the face(s) of some' — 'alcun' here = some of them and the article 'la' marks 'faccia'.
segments.inferno-31-g47: A simple coordinate list of body parts: 'le spalle e 'l petto' (the shoulders and the chest) 'e del ventre gran parte' (and a large part of the belly). 'gran' is the aphetic form of 'grande' used before a noun.
segments.inferno-31-g48: 'e per le coste giù ambo le braccia' = 'and down along the ribs both arms'. 'Per le coste' = 'along/over the ribs', 'giù' = 'down', 'ambo' = 'both'. Word order places the adverb and phrase before the noun ('le braccia').
segments.inferno-31-g49: 'Natura certo' = 'Nature, certainly' — 'certo' is an adverb/parenthetical intensifier modifying the whole statement that follows.
segments.inferno-31-g50: 'quando lasciò l’arte di sì fatti animali' = 'when [she] left/abandoned the art of making such animals'. 'Lasciò' is the passato remoto (simple past) of 'lasciare' used here to mean 'gave up/abandoned'; 'l’arte' = 'the craft/skill'; 'di sì fatti animali' = 'of such made animals' (i.e., creatures produced by art).
segments.inferno-31-g51: 'assai fé bene' = 'did very well' ('fé' = past of 'fare'); 'per tòrre tali essecutori a Marte' = 'in order to take/remove such executioners from Mars' — 'per' introduces purpose ('in order to'), 'tòrre' = infinitive 'to take away', 'essecutori' = agents/executioners, 'a Marte' = 'from Mars' (i.e., deprive Mars of them).
segments.inferno-31-g130: Così is an adverb placed first for emphasis meaning 'thus' or 'so'. Dis­se is the passato remoto (simple past) of dire, 'said'. ’l is the elided form of il ('the') before a vowel, and maestro means 'master' or 'teacher' (here Virgil as Dante's master); the whole unit is a compact, poetically inverted clause 'Thus said the master.'
segments.inferno-31-g131: E = 'and'. Quelli is a demonstrative pronoun 'that one' (referring to the previously mentioned creature). In fretta is a fixed adverbial phrase 'in haste' (in + fretta). Le man = le mani, the plural direct object 'the hands'; distese is the passato remoto of distendere, here 'stretched out' or 'extended'.
segments.inferno-31-g132: E = 'and'. Prese is the passato remoto of prendere, 'seized' or 'took'. ’l is the elided definite article 'the' and duca here means 'guide' (not a noble duke) — 'my guide' — with mio 'my' as a possessive adjective. Word order is normal S + V + O for this clause.
segments.inferno-31-g133: Ond' is a contracted form (from onde/ond) meaning 'whereupon' or 'by which', introducing the consequence. Ercule = 'Hercules' (Italian form). Sentì is passato remoto of sentire, here 'felt' (physical sensation). Già = 'already' emphasizing immediacy. Grande stretta: grande modifies stretta; stretta (feminine) functions as a noun here meaning a 'tightness' or 'squeeze' — 'a great squeeze.'
segments.inferno-31-g134: Virgilio is simply the proper name 'Virgil' set off by the following comma; no grammar point beyond naming the speaker/actor.
segments.inferno-31-g135: Quando introduces a temporal clause 'when'. Prender is the infinitive 'to take/seize' and is governed by the impersonal reflexive construction sentirsi + infinitive: prender si sentio means 'he felt himself able to take/seize'. Si is the reflexive pronoun 'himself'. Sentio (archaic spelling of sentì) is the past tense 'he felt'.
segments.inferno-31-g136: Disse is passato remoto 'said', a + me is the prepositional complement 'to me': 'said to me'. The phrase marks reported speech.
segments.inferno-31-g137: Fatti is an imperative form from farsi (literally 'make yourself' or colloquially 'stand/put yourself') addressed to Dante; qua is the adverb 'here' or 'right here'. Together: 'Stand here' or 'Make yourself here.' The form is typical of older/poetic Italian.
segments.inferno-31-g138: Sì ch' is the result/consecutive connector 'so that' and governs a subjunctive clause. Io is the subject 'I', ti the object 'you', and prenda is the present subjunctive of prendere ('that I may take you' / 'so that I can take you'). The clause expresses purpose/result.
segments.inferno-31-g139: Poi 'then'. Fece is passato remoto of fare, here 'he made/did' in the sense 'he arranged/made it so'. Sì ch' again introduces the result. Un fascio era elli e io is a poetic inversion: literally 'one bundle was he and I' — era is the imperfect descriptive 'were' describing the resulting state, elli e io = 'he and I'. The whole means 'then he arranged matters so that he and I were like a single bundle.'
segments.inferno-31-g140: Qual begins a simile ('as' / 'such as') and here introduces how something 'pare' (parere, present indicative 'seems' or 'appears'). a riguardar is the idiomatic preposition + infinitive meaning 'to one who looks' or 'to behold'; la Carisenda is the tower's name; sotto 'l chinato = sotto + article + past participle 'chinato' used adjectivally 'beneath the leaning (one)'. The clause is a descriptive subordinate simile.
segments.inferno-31-g141: quando 'when' introduces a temporal clause; un nuvol 'a cloud' is the subject; vada is present subjunctive (vada) used in a general/descriptive temporal clause 'when a cloud goes'; sovr' is the elided form of sovra 'over'; essa 'it' refers to the tower; sì 'so' (adverb) prepares the result clause introduced by che(d).
segments.inferno-31-g142: ched (archaic che + d) introduces the result clause 'so that'; ella 'it' again refers to the tower; incontro here is an adverb meaning 'facing / toward the onlooker' (the provided sense list doesn't include this spatial adverbial sense, so no matching index); penda is subjunctive present of pendere 'hang', used after che to express the resulting appearance.
segments.inferno-31-g143: tal 'such' refers back to the preceding simile; parve is passato remoto of parere 'seemed' (to me); Antëo is the proper name 'Antaeus'; a me is the dative 'to me' (english 'to my eyes' = 'to me'); che introduces a relative clause 'who/that was standing on guard'; stava a bada is an idiom 'stood on the watch / stood ready' (stare + a + noun); di vederlo chinare = di + infinitive expresses purpose/intention: 'to see him stoop' (vederlo = 'see him', chinare = 'bend/stoop').
segments.inferno-31-g144: e 'and' links the final clause; fu is passato remoto of essere 'it was'; tal ora 'such a moment/time'; ch' is contraction of che 'that'; i' in ch'i' is the contracted io 'I' (the provided senses for i do not match this pronoun, so index -1); avrei voluto is the conditional perfect of volere 'I would have wished / I would have liked' used here to express a past desire in the narrative ('I would have liked to go'); ir per altra strada = 'to go by another road' (ir = infinitive 'to go', per altra strada = 'by another way').
segments.inferno-31-g145: Ma is the coordinating adversative 'but'. Lievemente is an adverb ('gently/lightly') modifying the verb that comes later; it, together with the following prepositional phrase, forms the manner/place complement. Al fondo is the prepositional phrase 'to/onto the bottom' and che introduces a relative clause (fondo che divora ...) modifying fondo. Divora is present indicative (literary/poetic present) meaning 'devours' and takes Lucifero con Giuda as its subject.
segments.inferno-31-g146: Ci is the clitic direct object pronoun 'us' referring to the narrators. Sposò is the passato remoto, 3rd singular of sposare; here it functions in a non‑marriage sense (poetically 'placed/laid/joined to') and takes ci as its object, so the whole means 'he set us down' (or 'he placed us there').
segments.inferno-31-g147: Né is the negative coordinating conjunction 'nor' continuing the negation begun or implied previously; it introduces the following clause that is negated.
segments.inferno-31-g148: Sì is the adverb 'so/thus' (intensifier) and chinato is a past participle used adjectivally 'bent/stooping'; together 'sì chinato' means 'so bent' or 'thus stooped' and describes the subject's posture when the action is considered.
segments.inferno-31-g149: Lì is the locative adverb 'there'. Fece is the passato remoto of fare ('made/did'); here it combines with dimora (a noun) to form the idiom fece dimora 'made a dwelling/took up residence'. In context, after né it means 'nor did he make his dwelling there.'
segments.inferno-31-g150: E 'and' links the action that follows. Come introduces a simile 'like/as'. Albero in nautical usage means 'mast' (not a generic tree) and in nave 'on/in a ship' gives the image; si levò is the pronominal passato remoto of levarsi 'to rise/lift oneself', so the clause means 'and like a mast in a ship he rose up' (i.e., straightened himself up).
segments.inferno-31-g17: 'Poco' is an adverb meaning 'a little' and modifies the verb that follows. 'Portäi' is the first-person past (passato remoto) of portare (here 'I turned/moved'), used narratively. 'In là volta la testa' is an adverbial phrase plus object: 'in là' = 'to that side/there', 'volta' = 'a turn', and 'la testa' is the direct object ('the head'), so literally 'I moved/turned the head a little to that side.'
segments.inferno-31-g18: 'Che' here introduces the consequent clause (roughly 'when'/'that'). 'Me parve' is a past-tense construction with the dative 'me' = 'it seemed to me' (parve = it seemed). 'Veder' is an infinitive governed by parve: 'to see.' 'Molte alte torri' is the object of veder: 'many tall towers' (molte quantifier before the adjective alte which agrees with torri).
segments.inferno-31-g19: 'Ond'' (short for 'onde') is a connective meaning 'whereupon'/'therefore'/'whence' that links the vision to the speaker's reaction. 'Io' is the subject pronoun 'I' that introduces the reported speech or utterance that follows the colon.
segments.inferno-31-g20: A vocative: 'Maestro' addresses the guide (Virgil). In narrative/vocative position, it names the person being spoken to; punctuation and capitalization mark it as direct address.
segments.inferno-31-g21: 'Dì' is the imperative form (tu) of dire — an imperative/command meaning 'say' or 'tell (me)'. It's used here to request information from the addressee.
segments.inferno-31-g22: This is a direct question: 'che' is the interrogative 'what' (here 'what [land]'). 'Terra' is 'land/region' (feminine), 'è' is the present of essere ('is'), and 'questa' is the demonstrative 'this', agreeing with terra. Word order places the interrogative first for emphasis: 'what land is this?'.
segments.inferno-31-g23: This segment is only punctuation (a period), marking the end of the previous line/clause; there are no lexical items to analyze here.
segments.inferno-31-g24: 'Ed' = 'and' (a simple connective). 'Elli' is an archaic/poetic subject pronoun equivalent to modern 'egli' = 'he' (here 'he said'); the supplied sense list given in the data does not reflect this usage, so it is treated as the archaic pronoun. 'A me' = 'to me', marking the indirect object/recipient of the speech.
segments.inferno-31-g25: 'Però che' introduces a causal clause here ('because'). 'Tu trascorri' is present indicative 'you pass/go' (second person singular). 'Per le tenebre' means 'through the darkness' (per = through + plural article le + tenebre). 'Troppo da la lungi' is an idiomatic expression 'too far from (here)' — 'da la lungi' = 'from afar' (Dante's older ordering and article usage).
segments.inferno-31-g26: 'Avvien' is an impersonal present meaning 'it happens' or 'it befalls.' 'Che' introduces the subordinate clause ('that'). 'Poi' = 'afterward/then.' 'Nel maginare' = 'in (the) imagining' where 'maginare' (infinitive) is used substantively ('in imagining'). 'Abborri' is second-person present ('you abhor/shrink from'); here the verb describes Dante's reaction — 'you come to shun/abhor (it).' The construction is idiomatic: 'it happens that ... you abhor.'
segments.inferno-31-g27: 'Tu' is the subject pronoun 'you.' 'Vedrai' is the future indicative of vedere ('you will see' or 'you will perceive'). 'Ben' is an adverb intensifier meaning 'well' or 'indeed' (i.e., 'you will clearly/indeed see').
segments.inferno-31-g28: 'Se' = 'if' introducing a conditional clause. The second 'tu' repeats the subject for emphasis. 'Là' = 'there.' 'Ti congiungi' is a pronominal verb (congiungersi) in the present indicative 'you join yourself' — here meaning 'if you go/approach/bring yourself there' (i.e., if you get yourself to that place/point of view).
segments.inferno-31-g29: 'Quanto' functions as an exclamative/interrogative adverb 'how much' or 'how greatly.' '’l' is the contracted article 'il' (= 'the'). 'Senso' here means 'sense' or 'perception/understanding.' 'S’ inganna' is the pronominal verb 'si inganna' — 'deceives itself' or 'is deceived.' 'Di lontano' = 'from afar' (di expressing source/distance). The clause means 'how much the senses are deceived from a distance.'
segments.inferno-31-g30: 'Però' here = 'therefore/so.' 'Alquanto' = 'somewhat/a little.' 'Più' = 'more.' 'Te stesso' is the emphatic reflexive 'yourself.' 'Pungi' is the imperative (or hortatory present) 'prick/goad/sting' (tu) — figuratively 'urge or chastise yourself' (Virgil telling Dante to be more exacting).
segments.inferno-31-g98: Ond’ is a contracted/poetic form of 'onde' meaning 'thereupon' or 'therefore' and is followed by the archaic pronoun 'ei' (he). 'rispuose' is the passato remoto (third person singular) of 'rispondere', meaning 'replied/answered'.
segments.inferno-31-g99: 'Tu vedrai' is the simple future (2nd person singular) of 'vedere' — 'you will see'. 'Anteo' is the proper name Antaeus. 'presso di qui' is a fixed locative phrase meaning 'close by here' (presso = near; di reinforces the locative). 'che parla' is a relative clause ('who speaks') with 'che' as a relative pronoun and 'parla' present indicative 3rd singular. 'ed è disciolto' links with 'and is disciolto' where 'disciolto' is a past participle used adjectivally meaning 'loosed/freed.'
segments.inferno-31-g100: This is another relative clause introduced by 'che' ('who/which'). 'ne' is a clitic referring back to something already mentioned (here 'us' or 'them' — 'of it/them'). 'porrà' is the future tense (3rd singular) of 'porre' = 'will put/place'. 'nel fondo' = 'into the bottom/depth'. 'd’ogne reo' = 'of every guilty one' (ogni/ogne = every; reo = guilty person/sinner).
segments.inferno-31-g101: 'Quel' is the demonstrative 'that' (or 'that which') and begins the noun clause. 'che' here is the relative pronoun 'which/that'. 'tu vuo’' is an archaic poetic form for 'tu vuoi' (present tense 'you want'). 'veder' is the infinitive 'to see' with the final -e dropped for metrical reasons (common in Dante).
segments.inferno-31-g102: 'più là' = 'further on' ('più' = more/further; 'là' = there). 'è molto' literally 'is much' (= 'is a great way off' / 'lies far on'). 'ed è legato e fatto' uses two past participles with the copula 'è' to mean 'is bound and made' (i.e., bound up / constructed in that manner). 'come questo' = 'like this' (comparative reference to the present instance).
segments.inferno-31-g103: 'salvo che' is a conjunctive phrase meaning 'except that'. 'più feroce' = 'more fierce' (comparative). 'par' is the poetic present form of 'pare' (3rd singular) meaning 'seems/appears'. 'nel volto' literally 'in the face', idiomatically 'in appearance' or 'in its look'.
segments.inferno-31-g104: 'Non fu' is the passato remoto of 'essere' with negative, meaning 'there was not' (here idiomatically 'never was'). 'tremoto' = 'earthquake' is the subject. 'già' here reinforces the negation (classically translated 'ever' or 'already' in older usage). 'tanto rubesto' = 'so violent/so forceful' — 'tanto' = so much/so, 'rubesto' is an archaic adjective meaning robust/violent.
segments.inferno-31-g105: This is a result clause introduced by 'che' ('that') and it takes the imperfect subjunctive 'scotesse' (3rd singular) after the negative past construction to express 'so...that (it would) shake'. 'una torre' = 'a tower'. 'così forte' = 'so strongly/so forcibly'.
segments.inferno-31-g106: 'come' = 'as' introduces the simile. 'Fïalte' is an archaic noun meaning a small bottle or phial. The phrase 'a scuotersi' uses the preposition 'a' + infinitive 'scuotersi' (to shake oneself) — here an idiomatic passive-like expression meaning 'is quick to be shaken'. 'fu presto' (literally 'was quick') completes the simile: the phial 'was quickly shaken.'
segments.inferno-31-g107: Allor (an adverb) = 'then' introducing the moment; temett' is the archaic apocopated past tense of temette (temere) meaning 'feared' (past). The subject io 'I' follows the verb for poetic inversion; più che mai is a fixed comparative phrase 'more than ever'; la morte is the direct object 'death'.
segments.inferno-31-g108: e 'and' begins the coordinated clause; non v'era is the impersonal past 'there was not' (v' = vi); mestier is a noun 'need' (here impersonal: 'there was no need'); più che la dotta literal 'more than the learned (one)' — syntactically the phrase states what need (if any) remained. Poetic word order places mestier after the copula.
segments.inferno-31-g109: s'io is a contraction of se io 'if I' (the given sense list doesn't include the conditional 'se', so no matching index); non avessi viste is a pluperfect subjunctive (avessi + past participle viste) used here in a conditional clause meaning 'if I had not seen'; le ritorte is the feminine plural object 'the twists/turnings' (ritorte = twisted things or coils).
segments.inferno-31-g1: Una medesma lingua is an emphatic noun phrase: 'una' (an/one) + 'medesma' (the very same). 'Pria' is archaic/poetic for 'earlier'/'first'. 'Mi morse' is passato remoto (morse, 3rd singular of mordere) with the clitic object 'mi' before the verb: 'the tongue bit me.' Word order (article-adjective-noun + adverb + clitic + verb) is normal for Dantean narrative.
segments.inferno-31-g2: 'sì che' introduces a result clause ('so that'). 'Mi tinse' uses passato remoto (tinse, 3rd singular of tingere) with the clitic 'mi' marking the affected person: 'it dyed/stained me.' 'l’una e l’altra guancia' is a paired expression 'the one and the other cheek' (two definite items coordinated after the verb). The elided article l' is the expected poetic contraction.
segments.inferno-31-g3: 'e poi' = 'and then.' 'La medicina' is fronted (noun phrase) and 'mi riporse' is passato remoto of riporre (riporse, 3rd sg.), with 'mi' as indirect object: literally 'it gave/returned the remedy to me.' The semicolon marks the end of this short narrative action.
segments.inferno-31-g4: 'Così od’ io' = 'thus I heard' (od' is a contracted/poetic form of an older verb for 'hear' in Dante; the form in the wordlist doesn't match this use). 'Che solea far ... esser cagione' is a subordinate clause introduced by che: 'solea' is imperfect of soleare ('was accustomed to'), followed by the infinitive 'far' + infinitive clause '...esser cagione' meaning 'to make ... be the cause.' The construction 'far X esser Y' is causative—'to cause X to be Y.' 'Prima di trista e poi di buona mancia' uses 'di' with adjectives to qualify 'mancia' (gift/tip): 'first of sorrowful [reward], then of good [reward].'
segments.inferno-31-g5: 'Noi demmo il dosso' literally 'we gave the back'—an idiomatic Dantean way to say 'we turned our backs' or 'we withdrew/turned toward.' 'Demmo' is passato remoto (we gave/turned). 'Al misero vallone' is the indirect object ('toward the miserable ravine'). 'Su per la ripa' = 'up along the bank,' where 'su' + 'per' indicates upward movement along. 'Che 'l cinge dintorno' is a relative clause: 'che' (that) + present indicative 'cinge' (it encircles) with 'l' = lo (refers back to 'vallone'), and 'dintorno' 'around.' The present 'cinge' is used to describe a general property (the bank encircles it).
segments.inferno-31-g6: 'Attraversando' is a present participle/gerund ('crossing' / 'while crossing'), describing how they moved. 'Sanza' is an archaic form of 'senza' = 'without.' 'Alcun' in this context means 'any' (negative/absence formula after 'without'), and 'sermone' means 'speech' or 'conversation' — so 'without any speech/word.' The participle phrase is adverbial, giving manner.
segments.inferno-31-g7: 'Quiv’' (quí/qui) is a poetic adverb meaning 'here.' 'Era' is the imperfect 'was.' 'Men che notte e men che giorno' is a comparative idiom 'less than night and less than day' (i.e., twilight). The structure expresses an in-between time by repeating 'men che.'
segments.inferno-31-g8: 'Sì che' introduces a result clause: 'so that.' '’l viso m’andava' literally 'the sight/face was going for me' — 'm’andava' is imperfect of andare with clitic 'mi' (m’), meaning 'my sight proceeded/extended only a little.' 'Innanzi poco' ('forward little') intensifies the limited visibility. The imperfect indicates a continuous state.
segments.inferno-31-g9: 'Ma io senti’' is the poeta's past narration: 'ma' = 'but', 'io senti’' is a contracted passato remoto (sentii → senti') meaning 'I heard.' 'Sonare' is an infinitive used as the content of 'heard' — 'I heard a high/loud horn sound.' 'Un alto corno' = 'a high/tall (i.e., loud or high-toned) horn.'
segments.inferno-31-g10: 'Tanto ch’' = 'so much that.' 'Avrebbe' is conditional (avrebbe = 'would have') used to express the hypothetical effect: 'it would have made every tone faint.' 'Ogne tuon fatto fioco' combines 'ogne' (every) with 'tuon' (tone/sound) and 'fatto' (past participle of fare) + adjective 'fioco' ('faint/dim'). The conditional + past participle here states what effect the horn's force would have had.
segments.inferno-31-g11: The single 'che' introduces the relative clause that follows — a connective 'which/that.'
segments.inferno-31-g12: 'Contra sé' = 'against itself'; 'la sua via' = 'its way/course'; 'seguitando' is the gerund of seguire ('following'). The gerund phrase modifies the horn/sound in the relative clause: 'which, following its (own) course against itself...' — a poetic description of the sound looping or reflecting back.
segments.inferno-31-g13: 'Dirizzò' is passato remoto (dirizzò, 3rd sg. of dirizzare) 'directed/turned.' 'Li occhi miei tutti' is an archaic order: 'li' (the, masc. pl.) + 'occhi miei' (my eyes) + 'tutti' (all) — i.e. 'my whole gaze.' 'Ad un loco' = 'to one place.' The subject is the sound (implied by the preceding 'che'), so 'which turned all my eyes to one place.'
segments.inferno-31-g14: 'Dopo' = 'after.' 'La dolorosa rotta' is a noun phrase: 'la' definite article + 'dolorosa' (sorrowful/painful) + 'rotta' (here 'rout' or 'defeat'); 'rotta' in medieval contexts often means a rout (military defeat). The comma sets up the temporal clause that follows.
segments.inferno-31-g15: 'Quando' introduces a temporal subordinate: 'when.' 'Carlo Magno' is the proper name (Charlemagne). 'Perdé' is passato remoto (he lost). 'La santa gesta' = 'the holy/sacred deeds/exploits' — 'gesta' refers to heroic acts or exploits; the phrase describes the historical/mythical context for the 'rotta.'
segments.inferno-31-g16: 'Non' negates the clause. 'Sonò' is passato remoto of sonare ('it sounded'/'he sounded'); here it refers to Orlando's horn/blast. 'Sì terribilmente' = 'so terribly' (degree adverb + manner adverb). The proper name 'Orlando' comes after the adverbial phrase for poetic/emphatic inversion: 'Roland did not sound so terribly.'
segments.inferno-31-g71: Cércati is a reflexive imperative (second person singular) formed from cercare + -ti, literally 'search yourself' and here idiomatically 'feel/feel around'; al collo is the prepositional phrase 'at/to the neck' (a + il → al) giving the location.
segments.inferno-31-g72: troverai is future indicative, 2nd person singular ('you will find'); la soga is the direct object 'the rope'; che introduces a restrictive relative clause where 'che' = 'that/which', '’l' (= il) is the clitic article referring back to a masculine animate referent, tien is present indicative 'holds/keeps', and legato is a past participle used adjectivally 'bound'.
segments.inferno-31-g73: o here is a vocative interjection 'O' addressing the addressee; anima is the vocative 'soul' and confusa is an adjectival predicate in agreement, meaning 'confused' or 'bewildered'.
segments.inferno-31-g74: vedi is an imperative ('see' or 'look'); lui is the direct object 'him'; che introduces a relative clause. The clause 'che ’l gran petto ti doga' is elliptical/poetic: doga here functions as a 3rd-person action on ti ('you') — in context it means something like 'fastens/presses/affixes' (archaic usage). Grammatically, ti is the clitic object referring to the addressee, gran petto ('great breast/chest') is the agent/topic, and the whole clause describes what that figure does to you.
segments.inferno-31-g75: Poi is an adverb 'then' or 'afterwards'; disse is preterite ('he said'); a me is the indirect object 'to me'. The whole is a simple reporting clause 'Then he said to me:'.
segments.inferno-31-g76: Elli stessi s’accusa is emphatic: Ellì (he) + stessi (self/very) make 'he himself'; s’ (reflexive si) with accusa (present indicative) yields 'accuses himself' or 'betrays/confesses himself' — a reflexive construction meaning he gives himself away or shows his guilt.
segments.inferno-31-g77: questi è Nembrotto is a present identificative 'this one is Nimrod'; per lo cui introduces a causal/genitive relative phrase 'because of whose' (per = by/for/because of, lo cui = 'whose'); mal coto is a fixed collocation meaning 'badly planned/evil intent' (mal = evil/bad, coto = intent/aim). pur un linguaggio nel mondo non s’usa literally 'even one language in the world is not used' — pur = 'even/still', un linguaggio = 'a single language', nel mondo = 'in the world', non s'usa = 'is not used' (reflexive/passive-like use of usare).
segments.inferno-31-g78: Lasciànlo stare is an imperative phrase: lasciàn(lo) is the plural/hortatory or emphatic form 'let him be/leave him alone' with the object clitic -lo attached, and stare is the infinitive 'to be/stay'; e non parliamo a vòto is a hortative negative 'and let us not speak in vain' (parliamo as a first-person-plural optative/hortatory).
segments.inferno-31-g79: ché così è a lui ciascun linguaggio sets up a reason: ché = 'for/because', così = 'thus', è a lui = 'is to him' (i.e. 'is to him such that'), ciascun linguaggio = 'each/every language'; come ’l suo ad altrui compares: come = 'as/like', ’l suo = 'his own', ad altrui = 'to others' — meaning 'every tongue is to him like his own is to other people.'
segments.inferno-31-g80: ch’a nullo è noto is a concluding relative clause: ch’ (che) = 'that/which', a nullo = 'to no one', è noto = 'is known' — 'which is known to no one.' The synthetic order (ch’ a nullo è noto) is typical of Dante's poetic compression.
segments.inferno-31-g81: Facemmo is passato remoto (we made/did) 'we made/undertook'; adunque = 'therefore/then'; più lungo viaggio = 'a longer/further journey' (più modifies lungo, forming 'longer'). The whole means 'so we made a longer journey.'
segments.inferno-31-g82: vòlti is the past participle/elliptical perfect of volgere (turned), here used absolutely/elliptically: 'having turned' or 'turned', followed by a + sinistra 'to the left'. The phrase describes a change of direction.
segments.inferno-31-g83: e al trar d’un balestro is a temporal/contingent phrase 'at the drawing (the release) of a crossbow' (al = a + il; trar = apocopated infinitive 'trar' = tirar/trarre 'to draw/launch'); trovammo is passato remoto 'we found/encountered'; l’altro = 'the other one'; assai più fero e maggio = 'much more fierce and greater' (assai più = 'far more', fero = 'fierce', maggio = comparative 'greater' in poetic usage).
segments.inferno-31-g84: A + infinitive ('A cinger') introduces an action in an older/poetic Italian pattern meaning 'to gird' or 'to encircle'. 'lui' is the pronoun object ('him') and the relative clause 'qual che fosse 'l maestro' uses the imperfect subjunctive ('fosse') after 'qual che' to mean 'whoever he was' (literally 'whatever he might have been'). The article 'l' before 'maestro' is the definite article attached to the noun.
segments.inferno-31-g85: A short parenthetical clause: 'non so io dir' is the normal negative present construction 'I do not know how to say' with subject pronoun 'io' placed for emphasis and the infinitive 'dir' (archaic spelling of 'dire'). Word order is emphatic but standard for Dante.
segments.inferno-31-g86: 'ma' (= but) resumes the narrative. 'el tenea' is an imperfect-tense verb (he held/was holding); 'soccinto' is an adjectival/participial quality describing how he was held (poetic/archaic). 'dinanzi l'altro e dietro il braccio destro d'una catena' gives two spatial complements ('in front of the other and behind the right arm') and 'che 'l tenea avvinto' is a relative clause where 'che' = 'which' and 'l' = 'him'; 'avvinto' is a past participle meaning 'bound/tied'. 'dal collo in giù' is a prepositional phrase 'from the neck downward'.
segments.inferno-31-g87: 'sì che' introduces a result clause 'so that'; '’n su lo scoperto' = 'on the exposed (part)' (’n su is an older contraction of 'in su'); 'si ravvolgëa' is a middle/reflexive imperfect verb ('it wrapped itself'/'it was wound') describing the chain's action; 'infino al giro quinto' means 'up to the fifth turn' with 'infino' = 'up to' and 'al giro quinto' the limit.
segments.inferno-31-g88: 'Questo superbo' is a demonstrative + noun ('this proud one'); 'volle' is the past tense (preterite/imperfect in archaic usage) of 'volere' meaning 'wished' or 'willed'; 'esser esperto' is an infinitival clause meaning 'to be tested/proved' ('esperto' here = 'tested'); 'di sua potenza contra 'l sommo Giove' is the object/complement specifying 'of his power against highest Jove' (contra + accusative idea = 'against').
segments.inferno-31-g89: 'disse 'l mio duca' is a simple past reporting clause: 'said my duca'. In Dante 'duca' often means 'guide' (Virgil) rather than a secular duke; pronoun 'mio' precedes the noun as possessive.
segments.inferno-31-g90: 'ond’' (short for 'onde'/'ond') is a demonstrative/relatival adverb meaning 'wherefore/therefore' here giving a causal/teleological link ('therefore'); 'elli' is an archaic 3rd-person pronoun (here 'he') used as subject of 'ha' (has); 'cotal merto' = 'such merit/desert' where 'merto' is an archaic form of 'merito'.
segments.inferno-31-g91: 'Fïalte ha nome' = 'He has the name Fïalte' (a naming formula). The verb 'ha' is simple present 'has' and 'nome' is 'name'/'title'. Here the proper name is fronted as subject.
segments.inferno-31-g92: 'e fece le gran prove' is a paratactic clause 'and he made the great trials' with 'fece' past tense of 'fare' and 'le gran prove' = 'the great proofs/trials'. 'quando i giganti fer paura a' dèi' is a temporal clause 'when the giants made fear to the gods' — 'fer' is an archaic past form 'fecero'/'made' and 'paura a' dèi' = 'fear to the gods' (i.e., terrified the gods).
segments.inferno-31-g93: 'le braccia' ('the arms') is the subject of the following relative clause 'ch’el menò' where 'ch’' = 'che' ('which/that') and 'el' is the subject pronoun 'he'. 'menò' here intends a past action 'he led/brandished' (archaic/dialectal past of 'menare') — the relative links the arms to the action he performed.
segments.inferno-31-g94: 'già mai non move' is an emphatic negative: 'già mai' intensifies negation with 'non' to mean 'now never' or 'no longer ever'; 'move' is present tense third person 'moves' (here meaning 'move' in the sense 'are set in motion' — i.e., they never move now).
segments.inferno-31-g95: 'E io a lui' is a simple, emphatic dativity: 'And I (said) to him', with 'a lui' marking the indirect object (to him) and 'io' fronted for emphasis.
segments.inferno-31-g96: 'S’esser puote' is a terse, archaic conditional expression equivalent to 'se possa essere' ('if it can be' / 'if it is possible'): 'S’' stands for 'se' (if) and 'puote' is an archaic modal form meaning 'can/is possible' (the infinitive 'esser' is 'to be').
segments.inferno-31-g97: 'io vorrei' is a present conditional/polite desire 'I would like'; the clause introduced by 'che' uses an optative/subjunctive idea — 'che ... avesser li occhi mei' — here 'avesser' is an archaic subjunctive/conditional form meaning 'would have' (i.e., I wish that my eyes would have experience). 'de lo smisurato Brïareo esperïenza' = 'experience of the boundless Briareus' with 'de lo' = 'of the' (poetic/older prepositional form).
segments.inferno-31-g52: E = conjunction 'and' linking to the previous statement. s’ella is an archaic contraction of se ella (‘if she’); d’elefanti e di balene uses the preposition di twice to mark the objects of pentere (repent of); non si pente is present tense with the reflexive clitic si (she repents herself = she repents), here functioning as a normal present indicative after the conditional se (s’ella) to mean 'if she does not repent'.
segments.inferno-31-g53: chi = indefinite relative 'whoever' or 'who'; guarda is present indicative 'looks at' or 'examines'; sottilmente is an adverb modifying guarda and means 'subtly' or 'acutely' — together: 'whoever examines/subtly considers'.
segments.inferno-31-g54: più ... e più ... = correlatives forming a comparative ('more ... and more ...'). giusta and discreta are predicate adjectives modifying the omitted subject ('chi'): 'more just and more discreet.' la ne tene is an archaic word-order: la (direct object pronoun 'it' or 'her'), ne (partitive/of it), then tene (archaic form of tiene, 'holds' or 'judges'); so literally 'he/she holds it of it' → idiomatically 'he/she judges/holds it (in that way)'.
segments.inferno-31-g55: ché = 'for/because'; dove = 'where' (introducing a circumstance); l'argomento de la mente = 'the argument/reasoning of the mind' (de + noun = genitive); s'aggiugne is reflexive/impersonal present 'is added' or 'joins itself' (s' + aggiugne); al mal volere e a la possa = 'to bad will and to power' where mal volere = ill will and la possa = 'the power/ability' (possa here a substantive 'power').
segments.inferno-31-g56: nessun riparo = 'no shelter/remedy'; vi is the clitic meaning 'to you' or 'for you' here (objects of poter far); può far = present 'can make/provide'; la gente = 'the people' (subject). The order places the negated noun first for emphasis: 'no protection can people (give) you.'
segments.inferno-31-g57: La faccia sua = 'his/its face' (feminine possessive after noun). mi parea uses the imperfect parea (parere) with dative mi 'to me' and means 'seemed to me' — an evaluative past description. lunga e grossa = two coordinated adjectives 'long and large'. come introduces a simile 'like the pine-cone of St. Peter in Rome.'
segments.inferno-31-g58: e a sua proporzione = 'and in proportion to it' (a + noun to indicate measure/ratio). eran is the imperfect of essere ('were'), used descriptively; l’altre ossa = 'the other bones' (subject complement) — normal past-description word order with predicate preceding or following the subject.
segments.inferno-31-g59: sì che = a consequence connector 'so that' (introducing result); la ripa = 'the rind/bark/outer part' (subject of the following relative clause).
segments.inferno-31-g60: ch’era perizoma = relative clause 'which was a perizoma (loincloth)'; era is imperfect 'was' giving a descriptive background. dal mezzo in giù = 'from the middle downward' (dal = da + il; in giù = downward), specifying the extent of the perizoma.
segments.inferno-31-g61: ne mostrava ben tanto = 'it showed of it/so much of it' — ne is partitive/'of it', mostrava is imperfect 'displayed/showed', ben tanto is an adverbial phrase 'quite so much' and di sovra = 'above' (indicating location). The clitic ne refers back to the flesh/outer part previously mentioned.
segments.inferno-31-g62: che di giugnere a la chioma = 'that to reach the hair' (di + infinitive giugnere used as a noun phrase 'of reaching'); tre Frison s’averien dato mal vanto = conditional perfect: s’averien (si + avrebbero) dato ('would have given to themselves') mal vanto ('a poor boast'); s’ is reflexive/reciprocal and the whole means 'three Frisians would have had little cause to boast of reaching the hair.'
segments.inferno-31-g63: però ch’i’ ne vedea = 'therefore/because I saw of it' (però ch’ = perché/for that; i’ = io 'I' as subject; ne = 'of it'); trenta gran palmi = 'thirty great palms' (palmi = unit of measure). dal loco in giù = 'from that place downward'; dov’ omo affibbia ’l manto = 'where a man fastens the cloak' (dov’ = dove, relative 'where'; affibbia is present/imperfect form 'fastens' or 'fixes on').
segments.inferno-31-g64: The quotation is a string of onomatopoeic/nonsense syllables — a magical or bestial cry. In Dante this is treated as non-lexical speech; almi, though a real adjective elsewhere, here functions as part of the cry. The line is meant to be heard, not analysed grammatically.
segments.inferno-31-g65: cominciò a gridar = 'began to cry/shout' (cominciò past simple/perfect in narrative), with the infinitive gridar after a. la fiera bocca is the grammatical subject placed after the verb (common inversion): 'the beast's mouth began to cry out.'
segments.inferno-31-g66: cui = dative relative 'to which' (referring to the beast's mouth). non si convenia = imperfect impersonal construction 'it did not befit' or 'was not fitting' (convenire takes a dative); più dolci salmi = 'sweeter psalms' (più modifies dolci). Overall: 'to which sweeter psalms were not fitting any more.'
segments.inferno-31-g67: E = 'and' linking this speech to the narrative; 'l duca mio' = 'my duke/guide' (the article + noun + possessive); ver' is an archaic contraction of verso/verso a meaning 'toward/to' and introduces the indirect address; lui = 'him' (indirect object reference) — the colon marks the start of direct speech.
segments.inferno-31-g68: Anima sciocca = vocative 'foolish soul' (anima as noun of address, sciocca an adjective after the noun). The comma introduces the imperative that follows.
segments.inferno-31-g69: tienti is an imperative form (archaic reflexive/imperative of tenere) 'hold yourself' or 'hold on'; col = contraction of con + il 'with the'; corno = 'horn' (instrument) — literal instruction 'grip the horn' or 'hold the horn' (instrumental phrase).
segments.inferno-31-g70: e con quel = 'and with that' (quel refers to the horn); ti disfoga = 'vent yourself' or 'relieve yourself' (disfoga = present imperative/indicative depending on reading, with clitic ti 'yourself'); quand’ = quando 'when'; ira o altra passïon ti tocca = 'anger or another passion touches you' (tocca = 'touches/befalls' in present). The clause is an imperative instruction to use the horn to vent emotions when they assail you.
segments.inferno-31-g110: Noi (explicit subject pronoun) + procedemmo (passato remoto, 1st pl. of procedere) gives a simple past narrative 'we proceeded'. 'più avante' is an adverbial phrase 'further on/forward'. 'allotta' is an archaic/poetic particle (≈ allora/thereupon), so the whole clause means 'we proceeded further on at once.'
segments.inferno-31-g111: 'e' coordinates with the previous clause ('and'). 'venimmo' is passato remoto (1st pl. of venire) 'we came/reached'. 'ad Anteo' is a prepositional phrase 'to/at Antaeus' naming the person reached.
segments.inferno-31-g112: 'che' is a relative pronoun 'who/which' introducing a clause about Anteo. 'ben cinque' is an emphatic numeral phrase 'as many as five' (ben = emphatic 'well/fully'). 'alle' here is an archaic form (from a + le) and functions as part of Dante's compressions/older morphology; its exact referent is elliptical in the line but grammatically it is a prepositional/articulated form.
segments.inferno-31-g113: 'sanza' is an archaic form of 'senza' meaning 'without'. 'la testa' is the noun phrase 'the head' that is being negated; this phrase modifies Anteo introduced by the preceding relative 'che'.
segments.inferno-31-g114: Uscia here is an archaic inflected form of the verb 'uscire' ('he/it came out' or 'issued out') used poetically; it is followed by the adverbial phrase 'fuor de la grotta' = 'out of the grotto/cave'. Note the older orthography and the verb's placement after the participial phrase.
segments.inferno-31-g115: 'O tu' is the vocative 'O you'. 'che ne la fortunata valle' is a relative clause: 'who in the fortunate valley' with 'ne' = 'in'. 'che fece Scipïon di gloria reda' is another relative clause 'that made Scipio heir (reda = erede) of glory'; 'fece' is passato remoto of 'fare' ('made').
segments.inferno-31-g116: Quand' is an elided 'quando' = 'when'. 'Anibàl co' suoi' = 'Hannibal with his (men)' ('co'' = con i). 'diede le spalle' is an idiom literally 'gave the shoulders' meaning 'turned their backs/retreated'; 'diede' is passato remoto of 'dare'.
segments.inferno-31-g117: Recasti is passato remoto 2nd sg. of 'recare' ('you brought' or 'you carried off') referring back to the 'tu' being addressed; 'già' = 'already'; 'mille leon per preda' = 'a thousand lions as prey' with 'per' indicating 'as'.
segments.inferno-31-g118: 'e che' links additional material ('and that/and who'), preparing the conditional/hypothetical clause that follows.
segments.inferno-31-g119: This is a conditional protasis. 'se fossi stato' is the past subjunctive (pluperfect sense) 'if you had been'; 'a l’alta guerra de’ tuoi fratelli' = 'in the high war of your brothers' (a l' = alla). The clause is counterfactual/hypothetical.
segments.inferno-31-g120: 'ancor par che si creda' is an impersonal construction: 'it still seems that one believes' (par = 'appears/seems', 'si creda' impersonal/ subjunctive sense 'is believed'). 'ch’avrebber vinto i figli de la terra' contains the conditional-perfect 'avrebber' (archaic spelling of 'avrebbero') + past participle 'vinto' = 'would have conquered/defeated the sons of the earth.'
segments.inferno-31-g121: 'mettine' is an imperative form (metti) with the enclitic pronoun '-ne' meaning 'of him/it' or 'him down' here; 'giù' is the adverb 'down'. Together: a command 'put him down.'
segments.inferno-31-g122: 'e non ten vegna schifo' = 'and let it not come to you as a disgust' (literally 'and let not to-you of-it come disgust'). 'ten' is an archaic/clitic combination (≈ 'te ne' = 'to you of it'), 'vegna' is present subjunctive/impersonal 'come/happen', and 'schifo' = 'disgust'. The whole is an admonition not to be ashamed/disgusted.
segments.inferno-31-g123: 'dove' = 'where'; 'Cocito' = Cocytus (the icy river/region); 'la freddura' = 'the cold' (freddura used poetically for severe cold); 'serra' is present indicative 'closes' (from serrare) — 'where Cocytus shuts in the cold' (i.e. where the cold is enclosed).
segments.inferno-31-g124: 'Non ci fare ...' is a negative command: 'do not cause to us'. 'ci' = 'to us'. 'fare' is causative 'to make/cause'. 'ire' here functions as the noun 'anger' (Dante's word choice/older usage) so the clause means 'do not cause anger to us toward Tizio nor Tifo' — 'a Tizio né a Tifo' = 'at Tizio nor at Tifo' (objects of the feeling).
segments.inferno-31-g125: 'questi' = 'this one/that fellow' (points to the person in question). 'può dar' = present indicative 'can give'. 'di quel che qui si brama' = 'of that which is desired here' with impersonal 'si brama' ('is desired'); literal structure = 'he can give of that which here is longed for.'
segments.inferno-31-g126: 'però' = 'therefore/so'. 'ti china' is an imperative or hortative 'bow/stoop yourself' (ti = reflexive 'yourself', china = 'bend'); 'e non torcer lo grifo' is a prohibition 'and do not twist (torcer) the grifo' (grifo = griffin or beak/snout), i.e. 'do not wrench/turn it aside' — a vivid physical admonition.
segments.inferno-31-g127: 'Ancor' = 'still/yet'. 'ti' (clitic) = 'to you'. 'può' present indicative 'can'. 'nel mondo' locative prepositional phrase 'in the world'. 'render fama' = 'render/give fame/reputation' — infinitive follows the modal verb and the clitic appears before the finite verb ('ti può render').
segments.inferno-31-g128: 'ch’el' is a contraction of 'che egli' = 'for he/that he'; 'vive' is present indicative 'lives'. The short clause gives the reason: 'for he lives.'
segments.inferno-31-g129: Coordinated continuation: 'e lunga vita ancor aspetta' = 'and a long life still awaits him' ('aspetta' = 'awaits'). The conditional-exception clause 'se ’nnanzi tempo grazia a sé nol chiama' = 'if/but unless, before his time, grace calls him to himself' where 'se' = 'if/unless' (introducing the exception), '’nnanzi tempo' = 'before (his) time', 'grazia' = 'grace/favour' (here divine grace that summons to death), 'a sé' = 'to himself', 'nol chiama' = 'calls him not' (negation built into the conditional sense: i.e. unless grace calls him beforehand).

sentences.inferno-31-s9: Then he kindly took me by the hand and said: "Before we go any farther, so that the matter may seem less strange to you, know that they are not towers but giants, and they are in the pit all around the bank, from the navel downward, every one of them."
sentences.inferno-31-s10: Just as when the fog dissipates, the gaze little by little makes visible what the vapor that crowds the air conceals; so, piercing the thick and dark breeze and drawing ever nearer toward the bank, error fled from me and fear grew in me; because, just as Montereggion is crowned with towers upon its round circuit, so the shore that surrounds the pit rose in towers to half a man’s height — the horrible giants, whom Jupiter of heaven still threatens when he thunders.
sentences.inferno-31-s11: And I could already make out some of their faces, the shoulders and the chest and a large part of the belly, and both arms hanging down by the ribs.
sentences.inferno-31-s12: Nature, certainly, when she abandoned the art of making such creatures, did very well in removing such executioners from Mars.
sentences.inferno-31-s33: Thus said the master; and that one, in haste, stretched out his hands, and seized my guide, whereupon Hercules already felt a great pressure.
sentences.inferno-31-s34: Virgil, when he felt himself able to take (him), said to me: "Stand here, so that I may take you"; then he made it so that he and I were one bundle.
sentences.inferno-31-s35: Just as the Carisenda appears to someone looking up at it from beneath the leaning (tower), when a cloud moves over it so that it hangs forward facing you: so Antaeus appeared to me as he stood on the watch to see him stoop, and it was at such a moment that I would have wished to go by another road.
sentences.inferno-31-s36: But gently onto the bottom that Lucifer with Judas devours he set us down; nor did he, so bent, make his dwelling there, and like a mast on a ship he rose up.
sentences.inferno-31-s5: I had scarcely turned my head a little when it seemed to me I saw many tall towers; whereupon I said, "Master, tell me, what land is this?"
sentences.inferno-31-s7: And he to me: "Because you pass through the darkness from too far away, it happens that afterward you shrink from imagining."
sentences.inferno-31-s8: You will see well, if you join yourself there, how much the senses are deceived from afar; therefore prick yourself a little more."
sentences.inferno-31-s25: Thereupon he answered: "You will see Antaeus close by here, who speaks and is freed, and who will put them into the depths of every guilty one."

sentences.inferno-31-s28: Then I feared death more than ever, and there was no greater need than the learned one, if I had not seen the twists.
sentences.inferno-31-s1: One and the same tongue first bit me, so that it stained both my cheeks, and then it returned the remedy to me; thus I heard that it used to make the spear of Achilles and of his father be the cause, first of a sorrowful gift and afterwards of a good one.
sentences.inferno-31-s2: We turned our backs toward the miserable ravine, up along the bank that girds it all around, crossing without any word.
sentences.inferno-31-s3: Here it was less than night and less than day, so that my sight went forward little; but I heard a loud horn sound, so much so that it would have made every note faint, which, following its own way against itself, turned all my eyes toward one place.
sentences.inferno-31-s4: After the sorrowful rout — when Charlemagne lost the sacred deeds — Roland did not sound so terribly.
sentences.inferno-31-s17: Feel at your neck, and you will find the rope that holds him bound, O confused soul, and see him who fastens the great breast to you.
sentences.inferno-31-s18: Then he said to me: "He himself accuses himself; this is Nimrod, because of whose ill intent even a single common language in the world is not used."
sentences.inferno-31-s19: Let him be and let us not speak in vain; for to him every language is like his own to others, which is known to no one."
sentences.inferno-31-s20: So we continued our journey farther, turned to the left; and at the drawing of a crossbow we found the other one much fiercer and greater.
sentences.inferno-31-s21: To gird him — whoever the master may have been, I cannot say — but he held him bound, with one chain before the one and behind the right arm of the other, which held him bound from the neck down, so that upon the exposed part it was wound up to the fifth turn.
sentences.inferno-31-s22: “This proud one wished to be tried against his power versus highest Jove,” said my guide, “and therefore he has such a desert (merit).”
sentences.inferno-31-s23: Fialte is his name, and he made great trials when the giants struck fear into the gods; the arms that he brandished never move now.
sentences.inferno-31-s24: And I to him: “If it can be, I would that my eyes had experience of the immeasurable Briareus.”
sentences.inferno-31-s13: And if she does not repent of elephants and of whales, whoever looks closely holds it more justly and more discreetly; for where the argument of the mind is joined to bad will and to power, no shelter can people make for you.
sentences.inferno-31-s14: His face seemed to me long and large like the pine-cone of Saint Peter in Rome, and the other bones were in proportion to it; so that the rind, which was a loincloth from the waist down, showed so much of it above, that three Frisians would have made a poor boast of reaching the hair; therefore I saw thirty great palms of it down from the place where a man fastens his cloak.

sentences.inferno-31-s16: And my guide toward him: 'Foolish soul, hold yourself by the horn, and with that vent yourself whenever anger or another passion touches you!'
sentences.inferno-31-s29: We proceeded further on at once, and came to Antaeus, who, without his head, came out of the grotto.
sentences.inferno-31-s30: “O you who in the fortunate valley that made Scipio heir of glory, when Hannibal and his men turned their backs, already carried off a thousand lions as prey, and who — if you had been in the high wars of your brethren — it still seems to be believed that you would have defeated the sons of the earth: put him down, and let it not be a cause of disgust to you, where Cocytus locks up the cold.”
sentences.inferno-31-s31: Do not make us angry with Tizio nor with Tifo: he can give of that which is desired here; therefore bend down and do not twist the griffin.
sentences.inferno-31-s32: He can still render you fame in the world, for he lives, and a long life still awaits him — unless, before his time, grace calls him to himself.
